brief: GAP/WHY: Hydroquinidine known to reduce arrhythmic events in Brugada syndrome (BrS); its antiarrhythmic mechanism and electrophysiological effect on the BrS substrate remain poorly understood; aim = determine in vivo effect of hydroquinidine on ventricular depolarization and repolarization in BrS patients. DESIGN/SETTING: Prospective in vivo human study (named QUIET BrS); baseline vs on-treatment paired comparison. POPULATION/N: 12 patients with Brugada syndrome. INTERVENTION/COMPARISON: Hydroquinidine 300 mg twice daily; assessed at baseline and on-treatment. MODALITIES: standard ECG, high-lead ECG, signal-averaged ECG, and electrocardiographic imaging (ECGI). MEASURES: ST-segment elevation, activation time, repolarization time, activation-recovery interval (ARI); computed for ventricles and right ventricular outflow tract (RVOT). Serum hydroquinidine levels measured; adverse drug events captured via medication survey. RESULTS (baseline vs on-treatment, all in RVOT unless noted): repolarization time 301.1 +/- 24.1 ms vs 348.8 +/- 28.3 ms, P<.001 (increased). Repolarization gradients 1.1 +/- 0.4 ms/mm vs 1.6 +/- 0.4 ms/mm, P<0.001 (increased). ARI 241.3 +/- 18.1 ms vs 284.8 +/- 21.5 ms, P<.001 (increased). Changes in repolarization/ARI greater in RVOT than rest of ventricles (regional variation). Activation parameters did NOT change significantly on-treatment. ST-segment elevation over RVOT: subtle increase 1.5 +/- 0.7 mV vs 1.8 +/- 0.8 mV, P<.001. Hydroquinidine serum levels did NOT correlate with electrophysiological changes or with adverse drug reactions. SAFETY: 1 patient developed frequent nonsustained ventricular tachycardia on-treatment. TAKEAWAY: Hydroquinidine primarily affects ventricular repolarization and action potential duration (reflected by ARI) in BrS, with regional variation showing more pronounced changes in the RVOT; minimal effect on depolarization/activation.
